title: The Backbone of Impact Evaluation Methods
text:
Welcome
The Backbone of Impact Evaluation Methods – Back to Basics is an open textbook on the quantitative evaluation of public policies.
The book starts from a simple idea: policy evaluation is not primarily about applying sophisticated estimators. It is about constructing a credible counterfactual.
This book is intended for graduate students, applied researchers, policy analysts, and public officials who want to understand how impact evaluation methods work, when they are credible, and how their results should be interpreted.

title: The Backbone of Impact Evaluation Methods
text:
How to read this book
Each methodological chapter follows the same structure:

The evaluation problem.
The key intuition.
Identification assumptions.
Estimation.
Diagnostics and validity checks.
Common mistakes.
A worked example.
How to report the results.

title: Preface
text:
Transparency as the Guiding Principle of This Book
This book is not intended to be a comprehensive textbook on impact evaluation and causal inference. Many excellent books and articles already provide rigorous and detailed treatments of causal inference, program evaluation, and econometric methods for policy analysis.
The purpose of this book is different. It is to go back to basics and offer a simple and transparent way of looking at the core logic behind the most widely used impact evaluation methods.
In recent years, transparency has become central to the debate surrounding machine learning algorithms. Complex predictive models are often difficult to interpret, and it is not always clear how an algorithm transforms data into predictions. For this reason, researchers and practitioners increasingly ask for models that are not only accurate, but also interpretable and transparent. At the same time, much less attention is usually paid to transparency in the context of policy evaluation methods. It is often taken for granted that causal methods are transparent by nature. Yet, as discussed below, this is not necessarily true for some of the most commonly used impact evaluation methods.
In a successful randomized controlled trial (RCT), the golden standard of causal inference, the logic of the comparison is usually easy to understand. Random assignment makes treated and untreated units comparable before the intervention: on average, the two groups are similar in all respects except for the treatment they receive. As a result, the untreated group provides a natural benchmark for what would have happened to the treated group in the absence of the intervention.
Similarly, in a regression discontinuity (RD) design, the role of the comparison units is relatively intuitive. Units just below and just above the cutoff are compared because they are assumed to be similar, except for treatment assignment.
This clarity is often less immediate in other widely used methods. For instance, in matching approaches, it is not always obvious which untreated units are actually used to construct the counterfactual, how much weight each of them receives, whether they are sufficiently comparable to treated units, and whether all treated units are retained in the final matched sample.
In difference-in-differences designs, the comparison group is often described simply as “untreated,” but this label can hide considerable complexity. Applied studies do not always make clear which untreated units drive the comparison, whether not-yet-treated units are part of the control group, and how much the estimate depends on specific groups or time periods.
The same issue becomes even more complex in instrumental variable approaches, especially when the instrument is non-binary, when multiple instruments are used, or when the empirical specification includes several fixed effects, covariates, and trends.
The lesson from synthetic control methods. One of the reasons why the synthetic control method (SCM) has been so influential is that it makes a data-driven counterfactual construction more visible. SCM relies on an optimization algorithm to choose a weighted combination of untreated units, and this procedure is not necessarily transparent at first sight. However, the method reports the weights assigned to each control unit and usually presents graphs comparing the treated unit with its synthetic counterpart. This makes it possible to see which untreated units contributed to the counterfactual, how much they contributed, and whether the synthetic control provides a credible pre-treatment fit.
This feature is not only useful from a methodological point of view; it is also pedagogically powerful. It reminds us that most impact evaluation methods ultimately rely on a comparison between treated and untreated units. They are not magic formulas that automatically identify the causal effect of interest; they are structured ways of making comparisons credible under specific assumptions.
This point is particularly important because in observational studies, we do not observe a ground truth causal effect. We observe what happened after a policy was introduced, but we do not observe what would have happened to the same units, at the same time, in the absence of that policy. This makes it difficult to compare evaluation strategies as we often compare machine learning algorithms for prediction. In prediction problems, models can be assessed against observed outcomes, and their accuracy can be compared directly. In impact evaluation, instead, the counterfactual outcome is missing. We therefore cannot simply run a “horse race” among methods and declare which one is empirically more accurate. For this reason, the credibility of an impact evaluation strategy depends mainly on the plausibility of its identifying assumptions and on the transparency of the comparison it constructs.
Lastly, it is worth emphasizing that, although we will focus mainly on the most widely used impact evaluation methods, which rely on untreated units to construct the counterfactual and estimate causal effects, this book will later discuss forecasting-based counterfactual methods. These methods estimate causal effects without necessarily requiring untreated units in the data and rely on a very different set of identifying assumption (see Section XXX).

title: Preface
text:
The aim of this book
Its aim is to explain impact evaluation methods by focusing on their backbone: the counterfactual logic, the role played by untreated units, the assumptions required for causal interpretation, and the reasons why apparently simple comparisons can be misleading.
The book is written for readers who want to understand the intuition behind impact evaluation methods before moving to technical details. It is intended for students, applied researchers, policy analysts, and practitioners who use causal methods or read studies based on them.
Formal notation is kept to a minimum and postponed to the end of each chapter. The emphasis is on interpretation, design, credibility, and transparency.
Throughout most of the book, we will focus on binary treatments, where units are either treated or untreated. This is the simplest and most common setup in impact evaluation, and many causal inference methods were originally developed with this structure in mind. Randomized experiments, matching, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity designs, instrumental variables, and synthetic control methods are often introduced by asking whether exposure to a policy or shock changes an outcome relative to non-exposure. The binary framework also makes the counterfactual logic especially clear: for each unit, we compare the outcome under treatment with the outcome in the absence of treatment. Later in the book, we will extend this logic to the more challenging setting with continuous treatment, where the relevant question is not only whether a unit is treated, but also how much treatment it receives.






ImportantThe main message



Impact evaluation is not primarily about applying sophisticated estimators. It is about constructing a credible counterfactual scenario
Different methods provide different ways of approximating the missing counterfactual, and each of them relies on specific assumptions that should be assessed case by case, depending on the empirical setting, the available data, and the institutional context.


The book therefore does not try to cover every method, every extension, or every recent technical debate. Instead, it focuses on the basic logic of the main approaches and on the practical questions that arise when we use them to evaluate public policies.
Before taking the first steps in this journey, I would like to thank my mentor, Professor Guido Pellegrini, who taught me much of what I know about public policy evaluation. I am also grateful to my co-authors: through many discussions with them, I have refined my understanding of the discipline. Finally, I wish to thank the many curious students and public administration practitioners whose questions and insights have encouraged me to explain key concepts more clearly.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.1 An example: the opening of a nursery school
Does the opening of a nursery school in a neighborhood increase local employment? Do subsidies aimed at improving workplace safety actually reduce occupational injuries? Do higher salaries attract better candidates to run for mayor? How many people died because of the COVID-19 pandemic?
These are all causal questions. They ask whether a specific policy, intervention, or shock produced a change in an outcome of interest. In the language of impact evaluation, we usually refer to this policy, intervention, or shock more generally as the treatment. The treatment may be the opening of a nursery school, the provision of workplace-safety subsidies, an increase in politicians’ salaries, or an unexpected and large shock such as the COVID-19 pandemic. The outcome may be employment, occupational injuries, the quality of political candidates, or the number of excess deaths.
But how can we answer such questions in a credible way?
Consider the first example. Suppose that, at time \(t_0\), a neighborhood has no nursery school. At time \(t_1\), a new nursery school opens. By time \(t_2\), we observe the employment status of parents of young children in that neighborhood.
Nursery schools may serve several purposes, from supporting child development to helping families manage care responsibilities. In this example, however, we focus on one specific mechanism: access to childcare may free up parents’ time and make it easier for them to participate in the labor market.
However, simply observing what happens after the nursery school opens is not enough to know whether the nursery school caused a change in employment. To answer this question, we need to think in counterfactual terms.
This is the central difficulty of impact evaluation. We observe the employment status of parents in the treated neighborhood after the nursery school opens, but we cannot observe what their employment status would have been, at the same time, if the nursery school had not opened. The counterfactual is therefore missing. The goal of impact evaluation methods is to find a credible way to fill in this missing information.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
NoteThe counterfactual question



What would have happened to parents’ employment in the same neighborhood if the nursery school had not opened?

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.2 Three intuitive comparisons
There are several simple ways one might try to estimate the effect of the nursery school.
Option A: We could use a before–after comparison. We could compare employment in the neighborhood before the nursery school opened, at \(t_0\), with employment after the opening, at \(t_2\).
Option B: We could use a cross-sectional comparison. We could compare employment in the treated neighborhood after the nursery school opened, at \(t_2\), with employment in other neighborhoods where no nursery school opened during the same period.
Option C: We could use a difference-in-differences comparison. We could compare the change in employment in the treated neighborhood between \(t_0\) and \(t_2\) with the change in employment over the same period in neighborhoods that did not receive a new nursery school.
Each of these approaches is intuitive. Yet each can also be misleading.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.3 Why simple comparisons may fail
Option A: A before–after comparison may confuse the effect of the nursery school with other changes occurring over time. Employment may have increased even without the new facility. For example, the local labor market may already have been improving before the nursery school opened. In that case, attributing the entire increase in employment to the nursery school would overstate its effect. Similarly, if an economic shock occurred between \(t_1\) and \(t_2\), we might wrongly attribute the consequences of that shock to the nursery school.
Option B: A cross-sectional comparison may also lead to wrong conclusions. Neighborhoods with and without a new nursery school may differ in many ways even before the policy is introduced. They may have different income levels, demographic structures, education levels, labor-market opportunities, or demand for childcare. As these differences are usually related to employment, then comparing the two groups after the nursery school opens would not correctly identify the effect of the nursery school itself.
Option C: A difference-in-differences comparison is the most sophisticated among these simple alternatives, but it still relies on an assumption that may not hold in this application. The key requirement is the parallel-trends assumption: if the nursery school had not opened, employment in the treated neighborhood would have evolved similarly to employment in the comparison neighborhoods. However, if employment was already evolving differently in the treated neighborhood before the opening, this assumption may fail. In that case, the estimated effect may still mix the impact of the nursery school with other pre-existing differences in employment trends.






NoteIdentification issues with simple comparisons






Note: This vignette illustrates why the simple comparisons discussed above may fail to identify the causal effect of interest. Before-after comparisons may confound treatment effects with underlying time trends; cross-sectional comparisons may compare inherently different groups; and difference-in-differences may be misleading when pre-treatment trends differ.










ImportantBack to basics



The question is not merely whether an outcome changed after a treatment. The question is whether the outcome changed because of that treatment.
To answer it, we need a credible counterfactual: a convincing approximation of what the outcome of treated units would have been in the absence of the treatment

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.4 Why observational data are challenging
Constructing a credible counterfactual is not easy, especially when working with observational data, as in the examples above. In observational settings, policies are not usually assigned at random. Nursery schools may open in neighborhoods with specific characteristics. Workplace-safety subsidies may be received by firms that are more proactive, more exposed to risk, or better informed about public programs. Salary increases for mayors may affect municipalities that differ in many other ways from those not affected.
For this reason, simply comparing treated and untreated units may not reveal the causal effect of the policy.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.5 Why randomized controlled trials help
Estimating the causal effect would be easier in a randomized controlled trial. Suppose, for example, that a large number of similar neighborhoods applied to receive a new nursery school, but budget constraints allowed the government to build only some of them.
If the neighborhoods receiving the new facility were chosen at random, then, on average, the treated and untreated neighborhoods would be comparable before the policy. This is because random assignment breaks the link between treatment status and the pre-existing characteristics of neighborhoods: richer and poorer areas, neighborhoods with higher or lower employment, and places with stronger or weaker demand for childcare would all have the same probability of receiving the new nursery school.
With a sufficiently large number of neighborhoods, the law of large numbers helps ensure that these characteristics are balanced between the treated and untreated groups. In that case, the neighborhoods without a new facility would provide a credible approximation of what would have happened to the treated neighborhoods in the absence of the policy.






NoteWhy randomization is powerful



Randomization is powerful because it makes the comparison group credible by design. Differences observed after the intervention can then be more plausibly attributed to the policy itself, rather than to pre-existing differences between treated and untreated units.
This is why randomized controlled trials are often considered the benchmark for causal evaluation.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.6 Back to impact evaluation methods
In many policy contexts, however, randomization is not feasible. It may be too costly, politically unacceptable, ethically problematic, or simply impossible because the policy has already been implemented.
Much of impact evaluation therefore deals with a practical question:






TipThe practical question



How can we construct a credible counterfactual when random assignment is not available?


The methods discussed in this book can be seen as different answers to this question. Each method tries, in its own way, to approximate the missing counterfactual. The key issue is not to choose the most sophisticated method, but to understand which assumptions make a given comparison credible in a specific empirical setting.

title: 1  Why Impact Evaluation?
text:
1.7 The counterfactual question
For each treated unit, we would like to know two outcomes:

the outcome observed under treatment;
the outcome that would have been observed without treatment.

The first is observed. The second is not. This missing outcome is the counterfactual.
Different impact evaluation methods differ mainly in how they approximate this missing counterfactual outcome.






ImportantRCT thinking in observational studies



Donald Rubin puts the point very sharply:

“Valid frequentist causal inferences from an observational study can only be obtained if you embed the observational study within a hypothetical randomized experiment. […] Otherwise you are just looking at computer output and implied associations, and making up, generally silly, although sometimes interesting, stories.”

This statement captures a central lesson of impact evaluation: even when we cannot run a randomized controlled trial, we should still think as if we were trying to approximate one. This means asking what the corresponding randomized experiment would look like. Who would be eligible for treatment? What exactly is the treatment? When would it be assigned? What would be the control condition? Which units would be compared? And which variables would determine, or predict, treatment assignment?
This way of thinking is useful because it disciplines the design of observational studies. It forces the researcher to move beyond the mechanical application of an estimator and to make explicit the comparison that gives the causal estimate its meaning. In this sense, RCT thinking is not only relevant for randomized experiments. It is also a benchmark for observational studies: it helps clarify the target causal question, the assignment mechanism, the role of untreated units, and the assumptions needed to interpret an estimate causally.
Source: Rubin (2022), Interview with Don Rubin.

title: 2  Counterfactual Thinking
text:
2.1 The fundamental question
The fundamental question in impact evaluation is not whether treated units improved after the policy. It is whether they improved more than they would have improved in the absence of the policy.
This distinction is the foundation of causal inference.

title: 2  Counterfactual Thinking
text:
2.2 The missing comparison
For a treated unit, the untreated potential outcome is missing. For an untreated unit, the treated potential outcome is missing. Causal inference is difficult because we cannot observe the same unit simultaneously under treatment and under no treatment.
This is sometimes called the fundamental problem of causal inference.

title: 2  Counterfactual Thinking
text:
2.3 Credible counterfactuals
A credible counterfactual should answer the following question:

Can this comparison group plausibly represent what would have happened to the treated group without the policy?

The answer depends on the context, the institutional setting, the data, and the assumptions of the research design.

title: 2  Counterfactual Thinking
text:
2.4 Design before estimation
A useful rule is: design first, estimation second.
Before estimating any model, the researcher should clarify:

Who is treated?
Who is untreated?
When does treatment occur?
What is the outcome?
What is the comparison group?
Why is this comparison credible?

title: 3  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
3.1 Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs)
The potential outcomes framework provides the basic language of modern causal inference. Its central idea is simple: to define a causal effect, we must compare what happens to the same unit under alternative treatment states.
The origins of this framework go back to the work of Jerzy Neyman on randomized agricultural experiments first published in 1927 (Neyman 1990). Neyman introduced the idea that each experimental unit has potential outcomes under different treatments, even though only one of them is observed. Later, Donald Rubin extended this logic beyond randomized experiments, turning it into a general framework for causal inference in both experimental and observational studies (Rubin 1974, 1978). For this reason, the approach is often called the Neyman–Rubin potential outcomes framework or the Rubin causal model.
Suppose we are interested in the effect of a binary treatment \(D_i\) on an outcome \(Y_i\) for unit \(i\). In the simplest case,
\[
D_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if unit } i \text{ receives the treatment},\\
0 & \text{if unit } i \text{ does not receive the treatment}.
\end{cases}
\]
For each unit, we define two potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(1)
\quad \text{and} \quad
Y_i(0).
\]
Here, \(Y_i(1)\) is the outcome that unit \(i\) would experience if treated, while \(Y_i(0)\) is the outcome that the same unit would experience if untreated. The individual treatment effect is therefore
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0).
\]
This definition captures the essence of causal inference: a causal effect is the difference between two potential states of the same unit. For example, if the treatment is receiving a public subsidy aimed at reducing workplace accidents, the causal effect for a given firm is the difference between the number of workplace accidents with the subsidy and the number of workplace accidents that the same firm would have experienced without the subsidy.
The problem is that we never observe both potential outcomes for the same unit. If the unit is treated, we observe \(Y_i(1)\) but not \(Y_i(0)\). If the unit is untreated, we observe \(Y_i(0)\) but not \(Y_i(1)\). The observed outcome can be written as
\[
Y_i^{obs} = D_iY_i(1) + (1-D_i)Y_i(0).
\]
This means that \(Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(1)\) for treated units, for which \(D_i=1\), and \(Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(0)\) for untreated units, for which \(D_i=0\). In other words, we observe only the potential outcome corresponding to the treatment status actually received.
The missing potential outcome is the counterfactual. This is why causal inference is fundamentally a missing-data problem: one potential outcome is observed, while the other must be inferred. Holland famously called this the fundamental problem of causal inference (Holland 1986).
Because individual treatment effects are generally impossible to observe, causal inference usually focuses on average treatment effects (discussed in Chapter 5).
The reason is that, although we cannot observe both potential outcomes for the same unit, we can observe different units under different treatment statuses. If treated and untreated units are comparable, the average outcome among untreated units can be used to approximate the missing counterfactual outcome for treated units, and vice versa. In this sense, causal inference replaces the impossible comparison of the same unit in two states of the world with a comparison between groups of units that are credible substitutes for one another.
In randomized experiments, treatment assignment is controlled by the researcher. In a successful RCT, treatment status is independent of the units’ potential outcomes. Formally, random assignment implies
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This means that treated and control groups are comparable on average, not necessarily unit by unit. The logic of randomization is therefore statistical. By the law of large numbers, as the number of units increases, random assignment tends to balance both observed and unobserved characteristics across treatment and control groups.
This also clarifies why randomizing only two units, one treated and one untreated, is not enough to make the RCT logic convincing. Even if treatment is randomly assigned, the two units may still differ substantially for reasons unrelated to the treatment. With only two units, there is no averaging that washes out individual idiosyncrasies or pre-existing differences.
With larger samples, instead, the average outcome of the control group provides an estimate of what the treated group would have experienced without treatment. Similarly, the average outcome of the treated group provides information about outcomes under treatment. In formal terms,
\[
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0].
\]
Randomization does not reveal the missing counterfactual for each individual unit. It solves the counterfactual problem on average, by creating a credible comparison group that reveals the missing average counterfactual.
This is why average treatment effects can be estimated even though individual treatment effects cannot be directly observed. RCTs are therefore often considered the benchmark for causal inference: by design, they solve the counterfactual problem for average treatment effects (Neyman 1990; Rubin 1978).

title: 3  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
3.2 Observational Studies
In many social science and policy settings, random assignment is not feasible. Individuals, firms, schools, or neighborhoods may select into treatment, or policymakers may target treatment to particular units.
In observational studies, treatment assignment is not controlled by the researcher. Treated and untreated units likely differ in observable and unobservable characteristics before the treatment occurs. These differences may affect the outcome and create selection bias. As a result, a simple post-treatment comparison of outcomes between treated and untreated units may capture not only the effect of the treatment, but also pre-existing differences between the two groups.
Rubin’s contribution was to show that the same potential outcomes logic can still be used in observational studies (Rubin 1974). The goal is to reconstruct, as closely as possible, the comparison that a randomized experiment would have produced. Different causal methods do this in different ways: matching, weighting, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, instrumental variables, and synthetic control all try to estimate credible counterfactual outcomes under different assumptions.

title: 3  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
3.3 Key Assumptions
The potential outcomes framework is not, by itself, an estimator. It defines causal effects and clarifies what must be observed or assumed in order to estimate them. In its basic version, several assumptions are especially important.

3.3.1 Manipulable treatment
The treatment must correspond to a clear intervention that could, at least in principle, be assigned or manipulated. This idea is often summarized by the motto: no causation without manipulation.
For example, attending a training program, receiving a subsidy, or gaining access to a nursery school can be interpreted as treatments. By contrast, characteristics such as nationality at birth or age are not treatments in the same direct sense, because they cannot be assigned by an intervention.


3.3.2 Consistency
The observed outcome must correspond to the potential outcome associated with the treatment actually received. That is,
\[
Y_i^{obs} = Y_i(1) \quad \text{if } D_i=1,
\]
and
\[
Y_i^{obs} = Y_i(0) \quad \text{if } D_i=0.
\]
Consistency connects the observed data to the potential outcomes. Without it, the notation \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) would not map clearly into the outcomes observed in the data.


3.3.3 Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption
A central assumption of the basic framework is the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption, usually called SUTVA (Rubin 1980). This assumption allows us to write only two potential outcomes for each unit: \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\).
SUTVA has two main components.
First, there should be no hidden versions of the treatment. The treatment must be sufficiently homogeneous across treated units. If some units receive a light version of the programme and others receive a much more intensive version, then the notation \(Y_i(1)\) is too vague. There is not one treatment, but several different treatments.
Second, there should be no interference between units. The potential outcome of one unit should depend only on that unit’s own treatment status, not on the treatment status of other units. This idea is often traced back to Cox’s discussion of interference between experimental units (Cox 1958).
Formally, in the simple version of the framework, we write
\[
Y_i = Y_i(D_i).
\]
This assumes that unit \(i\)’s outcome depends only on \(D_i\). If instead unit \(i\)’s outcome also depends on whether other units are treated, the notation would need to be expanded.






WarningWhen SUTVA may fail



SUTVA may fail when the treatment has different versions or intensities, when treated units affect untreated units, or when there are spillovers, peer effects, displacement effects, or general equilibrium effects.


SUTVA can fail in two main ways: i) Different treatment among treated units. Suppose that a school introduces a tutoring program for students with low test scores. Some students receive one-to-one tutoring, while others receive group tutoring. If we treat all participants as if they had received the same intervention, we are mixing different versions of the treatment. In this case, the estimated effect would not refer to a clearly defined program. ii) The treatment received by one unit may affect the outcomes of other units. Suppose that students who receive tutoring share materials, explain exercises, or study together with classmates who were not selected for the program. In this case, untreated students are no longer unaffected by the intervention: their outcomes may improve because of their interaction with treated students. This violates the no-interference component of SUTVA. The comparison between tutored and non-tutored students would therefore be contaminated: the control group is not simply untreated, but indirectly exposed to the treatment.


3.3.4 Assignment mechanism
To estimate causal effects, we also need assumptions about how treatment is assigned. In randomized experiments, assignment is random:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This means that treatment status is independent of the potential outcomes. In other words, units with high or low values of \(Y_i(1)\) or \(Y_i(0)\) are not systematically more likely to be assigned to treatment. As a result, the treated and control groups are comparable on average, and the control group can be used to estimate the missing counterfactual outcome for the treated group.
In observational studies, this is usually not true. For instance, the key identifying assumption of matching methods is the conditional independence assumpion:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i \mid X_i.
\]
This means that, after conditioning on observed covariates \(X_i\), treatment assignment is as good as random. Under this assumption, treated and untreated units with the same values of \(X_i\) can be compared to estimate causal effects.
It is important to recognize that this assumption should generally be considered quite strong. It requires that all variables affecting both treatment assignment and potential outcomes are observed and correctly accounted for.
In other words, after conditioning on \(X_i\), there should be no remaining systematic differences between treated and untreated units that are related to the outcome. This is demanding because, in observational studies, treatment assignment often depends on factors that are difficult to measure. For example, workers may enter a training program because they are more motivated, firms may apply for subsidies because they have better managers, and neighborhoods may receive a policy because policymakers expect them to respond more strongly.
If these factors are unobserved and also affect the outcome, then treated and untreated units are still not comparable after conditioning on \(X_i\). In that case, the Conditional Independence Assumption fails, and the estimated treatment effect may still reflect selection bias rather than the causal effect of the treatment.


3.3.5 Overlap
Finally, for comparisons to be meaningful, treated and untreated units must be comparable. This is the overlap or common support condition.
The idea is simple: for the kinds of units we want to compare, we must observe both treated and untreated cases. If a certain type of unit is always treated, then the data contain no untreated unit of that same type that can be used to approximate its counterfactual outcome. Similarly, if a certain type of unit is never treated, then we cannot learn what treatment would have done for that type of unit from the observed data.
For example, suppose a subsidy is given only to very large firms, while the control group contains only small and medium-sized firms. In that case, the treated firms have no comparable untreated firms. Any comparison between the two groups would mix the effect of the subsidy with pre-existing differences in firm size, resources, and growth prospects.
Overlap therefore asks whether treated and untreated units occupy the same region of the covariate space. When this condition fails, causal inference becomes an extrapolation exercise rather than a comparison between similar units. A common response is to restrict the analysis to the region of common support, where treated and untreated units are actually comparable. However, this generally changes the causal parameter that can be retrieved.

title: 3  The Potential Outcomes Framework
text:
3.4 Summary
The potential outcomes framework defines causal effects by comparing potential outcomes under treatment and no treatment. Its main contribution is to make the counterfactual problem explicit.
For each unit, the individual treatment effect is
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0),
\]
but only one of these two outcomes is observed. This makes individual causal effects generally impossible to measure directly.
Randomized experiments solve this problem by making treatment assignment independent of potential outcomes. Observational studies try to approximate this logic using assumptions and research designs that make treated and untreated units comparable.
The basic framework relies especially on SUTVA: the treatment must be well defined, and one unit’s outcome must not depend on the treatment status of other units. When these conditions fail, the simple notation \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) may no longer be sufficient, and the causal question must be reformulated more carefully.






TipKey idea



Causal inference is not just about comparing outcomes.
It is about comparing observed outcomes with credible counterfactual outcomes.








NoteThe ladder of causality



Pearl and Mackenzie (2018) describe causal reasoning as a ladder of causality with three levels.



Note: The ladder of causality distinguishes between observing associations, evaluating interventions, and reasoning about counterfactuals. The potential outcomes framework operates at the counterfactual level: it asks what would have happened to the same unit under a different treatment status.


The first level is association. At this level, we ask whether two variables move together. For example: are firms that receive public subsidies less likely to have workplace accidents? This is an associational question. It can be answered by comparing observed accident rates, but it does not tell us whether the subsidy caused the difference.
The second level is intervention. At this level, we ask what would happen if we actively changed something. For example: what would happen to workplace accidents if a firm received a public subsidy for safety investments? This is the level of causal effects and policy evaluation.
The third level is counterfactuals. At this level, we ask what would have happened under an alternative state of the world. For example: for a firm that received the subsidy, how many workplace accidents would have occurred if that same firm had not received it? This is the level of potential outcomes, because it compares the observed outcome with the missing counterfactual outcome.
In this sense, the potential outcomes framework works at the counterfactual level of the ladder. It defines causal effects by comparing what happened with what would have happened under a different treatment status.








Cox, David R. 1958. Planning of Experiments. John Wiley & Sons.


Holland, Paul W. 1986. “Statistics and Causal Inference.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 81 (396): 945–60. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1986.10478354.

title: 4  Causal Parameters of Interest
text:
The starting point of causal inference is the idea that a treatment can modify an outcome for a given unit. Let \(D_i\) denote treatment status for unit \(i\), with \(D_i=1\) if the unit is treated and \(D_i=0\) otherwise. For each unit, we define two potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(1) \quad \text{and} \quad Y_i(0),
\]
where \(Y_i(1)\) is the outcome that unit \(i\) would experience under treatment, and \(Y_i(0)\) is the outcome that the same unit would experience without treatment.
The individual treatment effect is therefore
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0).
\]
This is the most direct definition of a causal effect: it compares the same unit in two alternative states of the world. For example, it asks how the employment status of the same individual would differ if she attended a training program rather than not attending it.
The difficulty is that, for each unit, only one of the two potential outcomes is observed. If \(D_i=1\), we observe \(Y_i(1)\) but not \(Y_i(0)\). If \(D_i=0\), we observe \(Y_i(0)\) but not \(Y_i(1)\). The observed outcome can be written as
\[
Y_i^{obs} = D_iY_i(1) + (1-D_i)Y_i(0).
\]
The missing potential outcome is the counterfactual outcome. This is the fundamental problem of causal inference: it is generally impossible to observe the causal effect of treatment for a single unit.






NoteThe fundamental problem



For each unit, we observe either \(Y_i(1)\) or \(Y_i(0)\), but never both.
Therefore, the individual treatment effect \(\Delta_i\) is not directly observable.


Because individual treatment effects are usually impossible to estimate, causal inference typically focuses on average causal effects. The most general parameter is the average treatment effect (ATE):
\[
ATE = E[\Delta_i] = E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)].
\]
Equivalently,
\[
ATE = E[Y_i(1)] - E[Y_i(0)].
\]
The ATE answers the question: what is the expected effect of the treatment for a randomly selected unit from the population?
However, the ATE is usually not the most relevant parameter for policy evaluation. A program is generally designed for a specific group, rather than for the whole population. In that case, we are often more interested in the average treatment effect on the treated (ATT):
\[
ATT = E[\Delta_i \mid D_i=1].
\]
Equivalently,
\[
ATT = E[Y_i(1) \mid D_i=1] - E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1].
\]
The ATT answers the question: what is the expected effect of the treatment for units that actually received the treatment? This parameter is especially useful when evaluating whether a program generated benefits for its participants.
The first component, \(E[Y_i(1) \mid D_i=1]\), is observed in the data:
\[
E[Y_i(1) \mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i^{obs} \mid D_i=1].
\]
The second component, \(E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1]\), is not observed. It is the mean outcome that treated units would have experienced without treatment. Estimating this counterfactual is the central empirical challenge.






WarningThe difficulties in estimating \(E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1]\)



A naive comparison between treated and untreated units estimates
\[
DIM = E[Y_i^{obs} \mid D_i=1] - E[Y_i^{obs} \mid D_i=0].
\]
But, in general,
\[
E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1] \neq E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=0].
\]
The difference
\[
Bias = E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1] - E[Y_i(0) \mid D_i=0]
\]
is selection bias. Therefore,
\[
DIM = ATT + Bias.
\]
This shows why a simple comparison between treated and untreated units does not necessarily identify the treatment effect.








NoteOther causal parameters: LATE and ITT



Besides the ATE and ATT, two other causal parameters are especially important in experimental and quasi-experimental settings: the local average treatment effect and the intention-to-treat effect.
The local average treatment effect (LATE) is the average treatment effect for the subset of units whose treatment status is changed by an instrument, assignment rule, or encouragement. These units are usually called compliers. In an instrumental variables setting, for example, the LATE is the effect of treatment for units who take the treatment when encouraged by the instrument and do not take it otherwise.
The LATE is important because many empirical designs do not identify the effect for the whole population. Instead, they identify the effect for the units whose behavior is shifted by the source of exogenous variation. This means that the estimated effect may depend on the specific instrument or assignment rule being used.
The intention-to-treat effect (ITT) measures the effect of being assigned or offered treatment, regardless of whether units actually receive or comply with the treatment. It is especially relevant in randomized experiments with imperfect compliance. For policy purposes, the ITT is often meaningful because it captures the effect of implementing an offer, eligibility rule, or assignment mechanism in the real world.
In short, the LATE focuses on the effect of receiving treatment for compliers, while the ITT focuses on the effect of being assigned or offered treatment.


In many applications, treatment effects may also vary across groups. We may then be interested in heterogeneous treatment effects, usually summarized by a conditional average treatment effect (CATE):
\[
CATE(x) = E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0) \mid X_i=x].
\]
For example, a training program may be more effective for younger workers than for older workers, or a childcare policy may have stronger employment effects among mothers with young children.
Similarly, one can define a conditional ATT (CATT):
\[
CATT(x) =
E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0) \mid D_i=1, X_i=x].
\]
These conditional parameters are useful because they tell us not only whether a policy works on average, but also for whom it works best.

title: 5  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
5.0.1 Random Assignment
A randomized controlled trial is the benchmark research design for causal inference. Its logic is simple: if treatment is assigned randomly, treated and untreated units should be comparable on average before the intervention. Therefore, any systematic difference in outcomes after the intervention can be attributed to the treatment rather than to pre-existing differences between the two groups.
In the potential outcomes framework, each unit \(i\) has two potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(1)
\quad \text{and} \quad
Y_i(0),
\]
where \(Y_i(1)\) is the outcome under treatment and \(Y_i(0)\) is the outcome without treatment. The individual treatment effect is
\[
\Delta_i = Y_i(1)-Y_i(0).
\]
As discussed earlier, we cannot observe both potential outcomes for the same unit. Randomized experiments solve this problem statistically: they create a treated group and a control group that are comparable in expectation.
Let \(D_i\) be the treatment indicator:
\[
D_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if unit } i \text{ is assigned to treatment},\\
0 & \text{if unit } i \text{ is assigned to control}.
\end{cases}
\]
In a randomized experiment, \(D_i\) is assigned by chance. This implies that treatment status is independent of the potential outcomes:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This condition is crucial. It means that treated and control units do not differ systematically in terms of the outcomes they would have had under treatment or under control. Any difference between the two groups is due only to random sampling variation.
As a result,
\[
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0],
\]
and
\[
E[Y_i(1)\mid D_i=1] = E[Y_i(1)\mid D_i=0].
\]
The control group can therefore be used to estimate the missing counterfactual outcome for the treated group.

title: 5  Randomized Controlled Trials
text:
5.1 A/B Tests






TipBox: A/B tests as randomized experiments



An A/B test is a simple form of randomized experiment, commonly used in digital platforms, marketing, and product design.
Users are randomly assigned to one of two versions of an intervention:

group A sees the original version;
group B sees a modified version.

For example, an online platform may randomly show two versions of a webpage. Version A has the original layout, while version B changes the color or position of a button. The outcome may be the click rate, conversion rate, purchase probability, or time spent on the page.
Because users are randomly assigned to A or B, the difference in average outcomes can be interpreted causally:
\[
\widehat{\tau}_{AB}
=
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{B}
-
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{A}.
\]
The key point is that the two groups should be comparable before seeing the webpage. Therefore, if version B produces a higher conversion rate, the difference can be attributed to the change being tested, rather than to pre-existing differences between users.
A/B tests are useful because they are fast, scalable, and relatively cheap. However, they still require careful design. The treatment must be well defined, users should be randomly assigned only once within the relevant experiment, outcomes must be measured consistently, and spillovers between users should be avoided.



5.1.1 External Validity
RCTs are strong on internal validity, but they do not automatically answer every policy question. An experiment may identify the causal effect for a particular population, period, and implementation setting. The effect may differ elsewhere.
For example, a training programme tested in one city may not have the same effect in another city with different labor-market conditions. A school intervention tested with motivated teachers may work less well when scaled up to a national programme.
This is the issue of external validity: whether the estimated effect generalizes beyond the experimental setting.


5.1.2 Summary
Randomized controlled trials provide the cleanest design for estimating causal effects. By randomly assigning units to treatment and control groups, they make treatment status independent of potential outcomes:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i.
\]
This allows the researcher to estimate the average treatment effect using a simple difference in mean outcomes:
\[
\widehat{\tau}_{RCT}
=
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{D=1}
-
\overline{Y}^{obs}_{D=0}.
\]
The strength of RCTs is that they solve the selection problem by design. The limitation is that they can be difficult to implement in social science and policy settings, and they remain vulnerable to practical threats such as non-compliance, attrition, behavioral responses, and limited external validity.






NoteKey idea



An RCT creates a credible counterfactual by random assignment.
The control group tells us what would have happened to the treated group, on average, in the absence of treatment.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.1 The Logic of Matching
Matching methods are among the most intuitive tools for causal inference with observational data. Their basic idea is simple: if treated and untreated units are similar in all relevant pre-treatment characteristics, then the untreated units can be used to approximate the counterfactual outcomes of the treated units.
Suppose we want to evaluate the effect of a policy on an outcome \(Y_i\). Let \(D_i=1\) indicate that unit \(i\) receives the treatment, and \(D_i=0\) otherwise. As usual, each unit has two potential outcomes:
\[
Y_i(1)
\quad \text{and} \quad
Y_i(0).
\]
For policy evaluation, we are often interested in the average treatment effect on the treated:
\[
ATT =
E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1].
\]
Equivalently,
\[
ATT =
E[Y_i(1)\mid D_i=1]
-
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1].
\]
The first term is observed:
\[
E[Y_i(1)\mid D_i=1]
=
E[Y_i^{obs}\mid D_i=1].
\]
The second term is not observed. It is the average outcome that treated units would have experienced without treatment. Matching methods try to estimate this missing counterfactual by finding untreated units that are as similar as possible to the treated units before treatment.
The logic of matching is close to the logic of a randomized experiment. In a randomized controlled trial, treated and untreated units are comparable because treatment is randomly assigned. In an observational study, treatment is not randomly assigned. Units may select into treatment, or policymakers may select units for treatment, based on characteristics that also affect the outcome.
Matching tries to make treated and untreated units comparable after the data have been collected. It does so by comparing treated units only with untreated units that have similar values of observed covariates \(X_i\).
For example, suppose we want to estimate the effect of a job training programme on earnings. Workers who attend the programme may differ from non-participants in age, education, previous earnings, motivation, or employment history. Matching would compare each treated worker with one or more untreated workers who are similar in terms of the observed pre-treatment variables.
A classic example is the evaluation of training programmes studied by LaLonde (1986) and later by Dehejia and Wahba (1999). LaLonde showed that estimating credible treatment effects with non-experimental data is difficult. Dehejia and Wahba then showed that matching methods, when implemented carefully and with rich pre-treatment information, can recover estimates close to experimental benchmarks.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.2 Conditional Independence Assumption
Matching is based on a key identifying assumption: the Conditional Independence Assumption. This is also called selection on observables or unconfoundedness.
For the ATT, the assumption can be written as
\[
Y_i(0) \perp D_i \mid X_i.
\]
This means that, conditional on observed covariates \(X_i\), treatment assignment is independent of the untreated potential outcome. In words: once we compare units with the same observed characteristics, treated and untreated units would have had the same outcome in the absence of treatment.
Under this assumption,
\[
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1, X_i=x]
=
E[Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0, X_i=x].
\]
Since untreated units reveal their untreated potential outcome,
\[
Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(0)
\quad \text{when } D_i=0,
\]
we can estimate the missing counterfactual for treated units using the observed outcomes of comparable untreated units.






WarningWhat matching can and cannot solve



Matching can adjust for differences in observed pre-treatment characteristics.
It cannot remove bias due to unobserved variables that affect both treatment assignment and the outcome.


This is the crucial limitation of matching. If treated and untreated units differ in unobserved characteristics, such as motivation, ability, managerial quality, or political connections, and these characteristics affect the outcome, then matching on observed variables is not enough.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.3 Common Support
A second key assumption is common support, also called overlap. For each relevant value of the covariates, there must be both treated and untreated units.
Formally,
\[
0 < P(D_i=1\mid X_i=x) < 1.
\]
This condition means that, for units with characteristics \(X_i=x\), treatment is possible but not certain. If all units with a given set of characteristics are treated, there are no comparable untreated units. If all are untreated, there are no comparable treated units.
For the ATT, we mainly need untreated units that are comparable to treated units. If some treated units have no similar untreated units, matching cannot credibly estimate their counterfactual outcomes.






NoteCommon support



Matching requires treated and untreated units to overlap in terms of observed covariates.
Units outside the common support should not be used for causal comparisons.


In practice, lack of common support means that some units are too different to be matched. Dropping these units can improve credibility, but it also changes the population for which the treatment effect is estimated.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.4 Exact Matching
In the ideal case, each treated unit would be matched with untreated units that have exactly the same values of the covariates.
Suppose \(X_i\) contains age, education, and previous earnings. Exact matching would compare a treated unit only with untreated units that have the same age, the same education level, and the same previous earnings.
The matching estimator for the ATT can be described in three steps:

Divide the data into groups defined by values of \(X_i\).
Within each group, compute the difference between the average outcome of treated and untreated units.
Average these differences using the distribution of \(X_i\) among treated units.

Formally, the ATT can be written as
\[
ATT =
E_X
\left[
E(Y_i\mid D_i=1,X_i)
-
E(Y_i\mid D_i=0,X_i)
\mid D_i=1
\right].
\]
This expression highlights the core idea of matching: compare treated and untreated units within comparable covariate cells, and then average over the treated population.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.5 The Curse of Dimensionality
Exact matching becomes difficult when the number of covariates increases. This is known as the curse of dimensionality.
If \(X_i\) contains many variables, it becomes increasingly unlikely to find untreated units with exactly the same covariate values as treated units. With continuous variables, exact matches may be almost impossible. Even with binary covariates, the number of possible covariate profiles grows very quickly.
For example, with 20 binary covariates, there are
\[
2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576
\]
possible covariate profiles.
This means that a very large dataset may be needed to find treated and untreated units in the same covariate cells. In practice, this makes exact matching infeasible in many applications.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.6 Propensity Score Matching
A major solution to the curse of dimensionality is propensity score matching, introduced by Rosenbaum and Rubin (1983).
The propensity score is the probability of receiving the treatment conditional on observed covariates:
\[
p(X_i) = P(D_i=1\mid X_i).
\]
Instead of matching units on all covariates \(X_i\), propensity score matching matches units on the scalar index \(p(X_i)\).
Rosenbaum and Rubin showed that, if treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes conditional on \(X_i\), then it is also independent of potential outcomes conditional on the propensity score:
\[
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i \mid X_i
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)) \perp D_i \mid p(X_i).
\]
This is the balancing property of the propensity score. Conditional on the propensity score, the distribution of observed covariates should be similar between treated and untreated units.






TipWhy the propensity score is useful



The propensity score reduces a multidimensional matching problem to a one-dimensional one.
Instead of matching on many covariates, we match on the probability of treatment.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.7 Estimating the Propensity Score
In practice, the true propensity score is unknown and must be estimated. This is usually done by modelling treatment assignment as a function of pre-treatment covariates.
Common choices include:
\[
P(D_i=1\mid X_i)
=
\beta_0 + X_i'\beta
\]
for a linear probability model,
\[
P(D_i=1\mid X_i)
=
\Phi(\beta_0 + X_i'\beta)
\]
for a probit model, and
\[
P(D_i=1\mid X_i)
=
\frac{\exp(\beta_0+X_i'\beta)}
{1+\exp(\beta_0+X_i'\beta)}
\]
for a logit model.
The covariates included in \(X_i\) should be chosen carefully. They should be pre-treatment variables that affect treatment assignment and are plausibly related to the outcome. If panel data are available, lagged outcomes can often be useful because they capture pre-treatment differences in outcome levels.
Importantly, variables affected by the treatment should not be included in the propensity score model, because doing so would condition on post-treatment variables and may introduce bias.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.8 Matching Rules
Once the propensity score has been estimated, treated and untreated units can be matched using different rules. Different rules imply different trade-offs between bias and precision.

6.8.1 Stratification Matching
In stratification matching, the range of the propensity score is divided into intervals, or strata. Within each stratum, treated and untreated units should have similar average propensity scores.
The treatment effect is estimated within each stratum as the difference in mean outcomes between treated and untreated units. These stratum-specific effects are then averaged, weighting by the number of treated units in each stratum.
If there are \(S\) strata, the estimator can be written as
\[
\widehat{ATT}_{strat}
=
\sum_{s=1}^{S}
\frac{N_{1s}}{N_1}
\left(
\overline{Y}_{1s}
-
\overline{Y}_{0s}
\right),
\]
where \(N_{1s}\) is the number of treated units in stratum \(s\), \(N_1\) is the total number of treated units, and \(\overline{Y}_{1s}\) and \(\overline{Y}_{0s}\) are the average outcomes of treated and untreated units in that stratum.


6.8.2 Nearest Neighbor Matching
In nearest neighbor matching, each treated unit is matched to the untreated unit with the closest propensity score.
For treated unit \(i\), the matched control is
\[
j(i)
=
\arg\min_{j:D_j=0}
\left|
\widehat p(X_i)-\widehat p(X_j)
\right|.
\]
The ATT is then estimated as the average difference between each treated unit and its matched control:
\[
\widehat{ATT}_{NN}
=
\frac{1}{N_1}
\sum_{i:D_i=1}
\left[
Y_i
-
Y_{j(i)}
\right].
\]
Nearest neighbor matching can be implemented with or without replacement. With replacement, the same untreated unit can be used as a match for multiple treated units. This often improves match quality but may increase variance.
A common extension is \(K\)-nearest neighbor matching, where each treated unit is matched to the \(K\) closest untreated units.


6.8.3 Radius Matching
In radius matching, each treated unit is matched to all untreated units whose propensity score lies within a fixed distance, or radius.
For treated unit \(i\), the set of matched controls is
\[
C(i)
=
\left\{
j:D_j=0,
\left|
\widehat p(X_i)-\widehat p(X_j)
\right|
\le r
\right\},
\]
where \(r\) is the radius.
The choice of \(r\) involves a trade-off. A smaller radius improves comparability but may leave some treated units unmatched. A larger radius increases the number of matches but may include poorer matches, increasing bias.


6.8.4 Kernel Matching
In kernel matching, all untreated units are used to construct the counterfactual outcome for each treated unit, but closer controls receive larger weights.
For treated unit \(i\), the counterfactual outcome is estimated as
\[
\widehat Y_i(0)
=
\sum_{j:D_j=0}
w_{ij}Y_j,
\]
where the weights \(w_{ij}\) decrease with the distance between the propensity score of treated unit \(i\) and untreated unit \(j\).
The ATT is then
\[
\widehat{ATT}_{kernel}
=
\frac{1}{N_1}
\sum_{i:D_i=1}
\left[
Y_i
-
\widehat Y_i(0)
\right].
\]
Kernel matching uses more information than nearest neighbor matching, but it may also rely on controls that are relatively far from some treated units. The choice of bandwidth is therefore important.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.9 Other Matching and Weighting Approaches
Several related methods follow the same broad logic of constructing credible comparisons based on observed characteristics.
Mahalanobis matching matches units using a multivariate distance measure based directly on the covariates. It is useful when the number of covariates is not too large.
Coarsened exact matching first groups covariates into substantively meaningful bins and then performs exact matching within the coarsened covariate cells. This can improve transparency and reduce dependence on model specification.
Inverse probability weighting uses the propensity score to reweight observations rather than to form matched pairs. Technically, it is a weighting method rather than a matching method, but it is based on the same idea: use observed covariates to make treated and untreated groups comparable.

title: 6  Matching Methods
6  Matching Methods
text:
6.11 What Bias Does Matching Remove?
Matching can address two important sources of bias.
First, it can address lack of common support by excluding treated units that have no comparable untreated units. This avoids comparing units that are too different.
Second, it can address differences in the distribution of observed covariates by reweighting or selecting controls so that they resemble treated units.
However, matching cannot address selection on unobservables. If treatment assignment depends on variables that are not observed and that also affect the outcome, matching estimates may still be biased.
In the notation used earlier, matching can help with bias due to observed differences between treated and untreated units, but not with bias due to unobserved differences.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.12 Main Limitations
Matching methods are useful and intuitive, but they have important limitations.
First, the Conditional Independence Assumption is often strong. In many observational studies, selection into treatment depends on unobserved factors. For example, firms receiving public subsidies may differ from non-recipient firms not only in observed characteristics, but also in managerial ability, expected profitability, or access to policy networks.
Second, common support may fail. Some treated units may be very different from all available untreated units. In such cases, the data do not contain enough information to construct credible counterfactuals for all treated units.
Third, matching methods may require large datasets. As the number of covariates grows, finding comparable treated and untreated units becomes increasingly difficult.
Fourth, results may depend on implementation choices: which covariates are used, how the propensity score is estimated, which matching rule is selected, whether replacement is allowed, and how common support is imposed.






WarningThe main caveat



Matching makes observational data look more like experimental data only with respect to observed variables.
It does not make treatment random with respect to variables that are unobserved.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.13 Empirical Illustration
An example of matching in applied research is the study by Bovan et al. (2018) on the effect of natural disasters on voting behavior. The authors study whether voters punish politicians for events outside their control, focusing on floods in Croatia.
Flooded and non-flooded areas may differ systematically. For example, they may differ in socioeconomic conditions, geography, political preferences, or previous voting behavior. A simple comparison between flooded and non-flooded areas may therefore be misleading.
The study uses matching to compare affected and non-affected areas with similar observed characteristics. Before matching, floods appeared to reduce support for incumbent politicians. After accounting for differences in control variables through matching, the estimated flood effect disappeared.
This example illustrates the core logic of matching: part of what looks like a treatment effect may actually reflect pre-existing differences between treated and untreated units.

title: 6  Matching Methods
text:
6.14 Summary
Matching methods estimate causal effects by comparing treated units with similar untreated units. They are especially useful when treatment is not randomly assigned but rich pre-treatment information is available.
The key assumptions are:

Conditional Independence Assumption: after conditioning on observed covariates, treatment assignment is as good as random.
Common support: treated units have comparable untreated units.
SUTVA and consistency: treatment is well defined and observed outcomes correspond to the appropriate potential outcomes.

Matching is attractive because it is intuitive, transparent, and closely connected to the logic of randomized experiments. Its main weakness is that it relies on selection on observables. If important unobserved factors affect both treatment assignment and outcomes, matching alone cannot solve the identification problem.






TipKey idea



Matching replaces an impossible comparison between treated units and their own missing counterfactuals with a comparison between treated units and similar untreated units.








NoteExample: evaluating a training programme



A classic application of matching methods is the evaluation of job training programmes. LaLonde showed that estimating the effect of training on earnings with non-experimental data can be difficult, because participants and non-participants may differ systematically before treatment (LaLonde 1986).
Dehejia and Wahba revisited this problem and showed that, under suitable data conditions and careful implementation, propensity score matching can recover estimates close to those obtained from an experimental benchmark (Dehejia and Wahba 1999). Their study became influential because it suggested that matching methods can help observational studies mimic some features of randomized experiments, provided that the relevant selection variables are observed.








Bovan, Kosta, Benjamin Banai, and Irena Pavela Banai. 2018. “Do Natural Disasters Affect Voting Behavior? Evidence from Croatian Floods.” PLOS Currents Disasters 10: ecurrents.dis.cbf57c8ac3b239ba51ccc801d3362c07. https://doi.org/10.1371/currents.dis.cbf57c8ac3b239ba51ccc801d3362c07.


Dehejia, Rajeev H., and Sadek Wahba. 1999. “Causal Effects in Nonexperimental Studies: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Training Programs.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 94 (448): 1053–62. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1999.10473858.


LaLonde, Robert J. 1986. “Evaluating the Econometric Evaluations of Training Programs with Experimental Data.” The American Economic Review 76 (4): 604–20. https://www.jstor.org/stable/1806062.


Rosenbaum, Paul R., and Donald B. Rubin. 1983. “The Central Role of the Propensity Score in Observational Studies for Causal Effects.” Biometrika 70 (1): 41–55. https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/70.1.41.
